title: STANDARD FOR NAMED VEGETABLE OILS CXS 210-1999

sections.section: STANDARD FOR NAMED VEGETABLE OILS CXS 210-1999
sections.summary: Titles the document as the Codex Standard for Named Vegetable Oils (CXS 210-1999).
Provides the standard's adoption and revision history (adopted 1999; revised and amended multiple times through 2024).
Notes 2022 amendments that revised Table 1 and Table 2 values for sunflower seed oil (fatty acids C18:1, C18:2, relative density, refractive index and saponification).
Notes 2023 amendments that modified Section 4 (Food additives), Section 8 (Methods of analysis and sampling) and the Appendix (Section 5 Methods of analysis and sampling).
Records a 1 Feb 2023 corrigendum correcting the fatty acid C16:1 value for pistachio oil in Table 1 from ND-0.2 to ND-2.0 to align with a prior Codex decision.

States 2024 amendments adding avocado oil, camellia seed oil, sacha inchi oil and high‑oleic soybean oil to Sections 2 and 3 and Tables 1–4, and amending Section 7.2 on labelling of non‑retail containers.

Corrigenda noting two corrections in 2025: the relative density for almond oil in Table 2 was corrected to 0.929 (28 Jan 2025), and footnote b in Table 3 was rewritten to clarify that the fatty acid values apply to oils for human consumption and may also be applied to corresponding crude forms (19 Feb 2025).
Scope: this standard applies to the vegetable oils listed in Section 2.1 when presented in a state for human consumption.

Description: defines the vegetable oils covered by the standard (including kernels, seeds, mesocarp sources, and oil fractions such as olein/stearin and high‑oleic variants) by specifying the botanical/source origin for each listed oil.

Product definitions: lists each edible vegetable oil and common synonyms and defines the botanical source or plant part (e.g., kernel, seed, mesocarp) and relevant fractions (olein, stearin, superolein) and high‑oleic or low‑erucic variants.

Other definitions: defines ‘edible vegetable oils’ as primarily glycerides from vegetable sources (may contain small amounts of other lipids), defines ‘virgin oils’ as obtained by mechanical procedures ± heat with only water washing/settling/filtering/centrifuging allowed, and defines ‘cold‑pressed oils’ as obtained by mechanical procedures without heat with the same limited purification allowed.

Essential composition and quality factors: compliance is based on GLC fatty acid ranges in Table 1 with allowance for national/geographic variation; the section also specifies numerical composition limits for certain oils (e.g., low‑erucic rapeseed ≤2% erucic acid; various high‑oleic minima and palm high‑oleic minimum) and slip‑point temperature limits for specified palm fractions.

sections.section: 3.1 Gas-liquid chromatography (GLC) ranges of fatty acid composition (expressed as percentages)
sections.summary: GLC fatty acid ranges and compliance: samples within the ranges in Table 1 comply with the standard; specific limits include low‑erucic rapeseed oil ≤2% erucic acid and minimum oleic acid contents for high‑oleic safflower (≥70%), high‑oleic soybean (≥65%), high‑oleic sunflower (≥75%) and palm oil with higher oleic content (≥48%).

Slip point: specifies temperature limits for palm fractions—palm kernel olein 21–26 °C; palm kernel stearin 31–34 °C; palm olein ≤24 °C; palm stearin ≥44 °C; (table also notes palm superolein elsewhere).

Food additives: antifoaming agents, antioxidants and emulsifiers listed in the General Standard for Food Additives (CXS 192‑1995) for category 02.1.2 are acceptable, but no food additives are permitted in virgin or cold‑pressed oils; flavourings must comply with the Guidelines for the use of flavourings (CXG 66‑2008).
Contaminants: products must comply with maximum levels in the General Standard for Contaminants and Toxins in Food and Feed (CXS 193‑1995) and with Codex maximum residue limits for pesticides.

Hygiene: recommends preparation and handling per the General Principles of Food Hygiene (CXC 1‑1969) and related Codex codes, and requires compliance with any microbiological criteria established according to CXG 21‑1997.

Labelling: requires labelling in accordance with the General Standard for the Labelling of Pre‑packaged Foods (CXS 1‑1985) with product names conforming to Section 2 and, where multiple names exist, the inclusion of a name acceptable in the country of use; labelling of non‑retail containers should follow CXS 346‑2021.

Name of the food: product labelling must follow CXS 1‑1985 and the oil name must conform to the descriptions in Section 2, with one of the Section 2 names used that is acceptable in the country of use.
Labelling of non‑retail containers: labelling for non‑retail containers should be in accordance with CXS 346‑2021.

Methods of analysis and sampling: compliance checks must use the methods of analysis and sampling in the Recommended Methods of Analysis and Sampling (CXS 234‑1999) relevant to this standard's provisions.

Quality characteristics: the oil’s colour, odour and taste must be characteristic and free from foreign or rancid off‑notes, and maximum limits are specified for parameters (e.g., volatile matter 0.2% m/m, insoluble impurities 0.05% m/m, soap 0.005% m/m) plus specified metal, acid value, free fatty acid and peroxide value limits that differ for refined versus cold‑pressed/virgin and for particular oils (e.g., virgin palm oil FFA 5.0% as palmitic acid).

Composition characteristics: sets compositional limits and identity tests—arachis oil arachidic and higher fatty acids ≤48 g/kg; Reichert and Polenske value ranges for coconut, palm kernel and babassu oils; Halphen test positive for cottonseed oil; grapeseed oil erythrodiol >2% of total sterols; and total carotenoid ranges for unbleached palm oil, olein and stearin.

Appendix: specifies additional identity/composition criteria—Crismer value 67–70 for low‑erucic rapeseed oil, brassicasterol >5% of total sterols in that oil, a positive Baudouin test for sesame oil, and gamma‑oryzanol 0.9–2.1% in crude rice bran oil.
Chemical and physical characteristics: chemical and physical parameters for the oils are provided in Table 2 (refer to Table 2 for specific values).
Identity characteristics: gives levels of desmethylsterols as percentage of total sterols in Table 3 and levels of tocopherols/tocotrienols in Table 4 to support oil identity.

Specifies a spectrophotometric method for determining gamma oryzanol (%) in crude rice bran oil for compliance (referencing CXS 234-1999), requiring ~0.02 g sample dissolved in n-heptane (25 mL), absorbance measurement near 315 nm in a 1 cm cuvette (acceptable A range 0.3–0.6), and calculation Gamma oryzanol % = 25 × (1/W) × A × (1/E) with E1%1cm = 359.
